issues.title: getRfis / getSubmittals have no error or empty state — callers get unhandled promise rejections
issues.description: buildPaginatedQuery throws on Supabase errors but getRfis does not catch or rethrow with a typed AppError. Any component awaiting getRfis receives a raw Supabase PostgrestError, which has no stable shape for UI error boundaries to inspect.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/rfis.ts, in the getRfis function: BEFORE: the function calls buildPaginatedQuery and lets any thrown error propagate as a raw Supabase PostgrestError with no wrapping. AFTER: wrap the buildPaginatedQuery call in a try/catch block; in the catch block call transformSupabaseError(err) and rethrow the result so every caller receives a consistent typed AppError with a message and status code. Also add a guard: if pagination is supplied and pagination.page is less than 1, throw a new Error('pagination.page must be >= 1') before the query executes. Apply the identical pattern to the getRfis return type — add an explicit return type annotation of Promise<PaginatedResult<MappedRfi>> on the function signature (it is currently inferred). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: schedule.ts SCHEDULE_SELECT constant is truncated — file ends mid-assignment causing a build error
issues.description: The SCHEDULE_SELECT constant declaration is cut off at the assignment operator with no value, closing semicolon, or rest of the file. This is a syntax error that will prevent the TypeScript compiler from processing the module at all.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/schedule.ts, in the module-level SCHEDULE_SELECT constant: BEFORE: the constant is declared as `const SCHEDULE_SELECT =` with no value — the file is truncated and the rest of the select-column string and all downstream functions (getSchedulePhases, updateSchedulePhase, etc.) are missing. AFTER: complete the SCHEDULE_SELECT string with the comma-separated column list that matches the SchedulePhaseRow type fields used by mapScheduleActivityRow: `'id, project_id, title, start_date, end_date, duration, percent_complete, is_critical_path, predecessor_ids, phase, status, assigned_to, created_at, updated_at'`. Then ensure all functions that reference SCHEDULE_SELECT — getSchedulePhases, getSchedulePhaseById, and any upsert helpers — are present and use this constant in their .select() calls. Each function must have an explicit async return type annotation (e.g. Promise<ScheduleActivity[]>). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: supabaseMutation cast to `any` in createRfi / updateRfi bypasses type safety on insert/update
issues.description: Both createRfi and updateRfi cast the Supabase table builder to `any` to call .insert() / .update(). This silences TypeScript's row-type checking, meaning mis-shaped payloads will compile fine but fail at runtime with a 400 from Supabase.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/rfis.ts, in the createRfi and updateRfi functions: BEFORE: both functions pass a callback to supabaseMutation that casts the table builder to `any` — `(client.from('rfis') as any).insert(...)` and `(client.from('rfis') as any).update(...)` — suppressing all Supabase type inference. AFTER: remove the `as any` casts. Instead type the client parameter in the callback explicitly as `typeof supabase` and let Supabase's generated types infer the row shape. If the generated Database types are not yet in scope, import the `Database` type from `../../types/supabase` and use `client.from<'rfis', Database['public']['Tables']['rfis']>('rfis')`. Also tighten CreateRfiPayload in the types/api.ts import so it matches the rfis table insert type (add `Omit<RfiRow, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at' | 'number'>` as its base). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: GenRFIResponse renders status badge and priority tag with no accessible text — screen readers get nothing
issues.description: The status badge renders an icon (AlertCircle, Clock, CheckCircle, XCircle) inside a <span> alongside a text label, but there is no aria-label on the outer span. The PriorityTag component receives a priority prop but the rendered element has no role or aria-label. A screen reader announces both as unlabelled graphics.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/ai/generativeUI/GenRFIResponse.tsx, in the GenRFIResponse component: BEFORE: the status badge <span> has no aria-label — it renders `{cfg.icon} {cfg.label}` but the icon has no aria-hidden attribute, so screen readers announce the SVG title alongside the label text creating a duplicate/garbled reading. The surrounding <span> has no role attribute. AFTER: (1) Add `aria-hidden={true}` to each icon inside STATUS_CONFIG values — change each icon element, e.g. `<AlertCircle size={12} aria-hidden />`, for all five status entries. (2) Add `role='status'` and `aria-label={\`RFI status: ${cfg.label}\`}` to the outer status badge <span>. (3) Wrap the days-open <span> with `aria-label={\`${block.days_open} days open\`}` so assistive tech reads a complete sentence. (4) Add `aria-label={\`RFI ${block.rfi_number}: ${block.question.slice(0, 80)}\`}` to the outermost Card element by passing it as an `aria-label` prop, and add `role='article'` to the Card. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: getSubmittalRevisions has no error handling and returns typed array but Supabase error path returns undefined
issues.description: getSubmittalRevisions destructures {data, error} but never calls transformSupabaseError on the error branch — the function signature promises SubmittalRevision[] but on a Supabase error it throws a raw object. The file is also truncated after the .eq() call, so the return statement and any revision mapping logic are missing entirely.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/submittals.ts, in the getSubmittalRevisions function: BEFORE: the function queries submittal_revisions with .eq('submittal_id', submittalId) and the file is truncated — there is no error check, no return statement, and no data mapping. Any error from Supabase propagates as a raw PostgrestError; any success returns undefined because there is no return. AFTER: complete the function body — after the .eq() call, add `.order('revision_number', { ascending: true })`; then add `if (error) throw transformSupabaseError(error);`; then add `return (data ?? []) as SubmittalRevision[];`. Add an explicit return type of `Promise<SubmittalRevision[]>` to the function signature. Also add the same try/catch + transformSupabaseError wrapping to getSubmittalById and updateSubmittal so all exported functions in this file surface typed errors consistently. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's RFI and submittal APIs surface structured error responses with error codes that map directly to UI messages — their SDKs never expose raw database errors to the client layer. Fieldwire's mobile-first approach ensures every list endpoint paginates from the start and every error has a user-readable fallback. SiteSync's current API layer is closer to a prototype than a production system: truncated files, any-casts, missing error transforms, and zero accessibility on AI-generated components would immediately disqualify it in an enterprise GC evaluation where PMs rely on assistive technology and the legal team audits error handling in claim scenarios.
summary: The core-workflows module scores 53/100. The most damaging issues are structural: the schedule.ts file is syntactically broken with a truncated SCHEDULE_SELECT constant that will prevent compilation entirely; submittals.ts is similarly truncated with getSubmittalRevisions missing its return statement and error handling; getRfis propagates raw Supabase errors with no typed wrapping; both createRfi and updateRfi use `as any` casts that eliminate the type safety Supabase's generated types provide; and GenRFIResponse renders status icons and priority tags with no ARIA attributes, making the AI copilot's primary UI component invisible to screen readers. None of these require new features — they are precision fixes to existing code that, once applied, will meaningfully raise TypeScript quality, error handling, and accessibility scores toward the 80+ range needed for enterprise readiness.
